coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the mechanical and electrochemical corrosion properties of Cu composites via manipulation of intragranular graphene quantum dots cluster
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenfu
Liu, Liang
Bao, Rui
Li, Caiju
Chen, Xiaofeng
Pai, Junjun
Liu, Chunxuan
Liang, Qiwen
coredata.dc:description:
Intragranular distribution of reinforcements is highly valued for simultaneously improving strength and corrosion resistance in metal composites. Here, we manipulated zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) clusters into Cu grain interiors via a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy. The uniformly dispersed GQDs clusters activate multiple hardening mechanisms resulted in superior mechanical strengthening efficiency (S
eff = 228). Furthermore, GQDs clusters contribute to induce the twin domains in composites via forming high stress field, which would elevate the grain boundary stability and in turn restrained the corrosion process. Compared with pure Cu, 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite has a lower corrosion current density (1.50 × 10−5 A cm−2), reducing the corrosion rate by 31.2%. Our work has provided a feasible route to broaden the Cu composites application as reliable structural components.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu matrix composites
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Intragranular reinforcement
Mechanical performances
Corrosion resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs nanoparticles and composite powders

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of GQDs/Cu composites fabrication process.

(a-b) TEM and HRTEM images of synthetized GQDs, with an inset corresponding to size statistic; (c) FTIR spectra of GQDs; SEM images of (d) as-fabricated GQDs/Cu2O powders; (e) GQDs/Cu powders after reducing by hydrogen; (f) lamellar GQDs/Cu powders deformed by BM.

EBSD images and grain sizes distribution of (a, c) pure Cu and (b, d) 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite (the blue plane of the insets (a, b) represents the sample surface and the direction of the black arrow represents the pressing direction during SPS); (e) a histogram of twin domain area rates; (f) DSC curves for pure Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Microstructure of 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite: (a-b) intragranular distribution characteristic of GQDs clusters; (c) size distribution of GQDs clusters; (d) HRTEM image of GQDs cluster-Cu interface, with inset corresponding FFT image of the white frame; (e-f) the IFFT images of white frame in Fig. 4 (d).
(a) Engineering tensile stress-strain curves of bulk samples; (b) radar map comparisons of electrical and mechanical properties; (c-e) TEM and IFFT images of post-deformed 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite.
Electrochemical responses of bulk samples: (a) module plot; (b) phase angle diagram; (c) EIS Nyquist spectra, with inset corresponding equivalent circuit proposed to fit the EIS; (d)Tafel curves.
SEM images of corrosion surface morphologies of (a) Cu and (b) 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite; schematic illustration for may corrosive pathway of (c) pure Cu and (d) GQDs/Cu composites.
Performance statistics of pure Cu and GQDs/Cu composites, at least three specimens were tested for each sample set.
Corrosion parameters of pure Cu and GQDs/Cu composites, at least three specimens were tested for each sample set.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the mechanical and electrochemical corrosion properties of Cu composites via manipulation of intragranular graphene quantum dots cluster

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Intragranular distribution of reinforcements is highly valued for simultaneously improving strength and corrosion resistance in metal composites. Here, we manipulated zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) clusters into Cu grain interiors via a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy. The uniformly dispersed GQDs clusters activate multiple hardening mechanisms resulted in superior mechanical strengthening efficiency (S
eff = 228). Furthermore, GQDs clusters contribute to induce the twin domains in composites via forming high stress field, which would elevate the grain boundary stability and in turn restrained the corrosion process. Compared with pure Cu, 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite has a lower corrosion current density (1.50 × 10−5 A cm−2), reducing the corrosion rate by 31.2%. Our work has provided a feasible route to broaden the Cu composites application as reliable structural components.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these finding cannot be shared at this time as the date also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Particulate-reinforced Cu matrix composites (PRCMCs) have been used in electrical equipment and resistance welding electrodes fields due to their excellent electrical and thermal properties [1]. In a drive to further ensure application reliability and service life, pursuing high strength and exceptional corrosion resistance are urgently required [2]. Theoretically, Cu composites with elevated comprehensive performance could be achieved by uniformly dispersed thermally stable second-phase particles in matrix grains owing to the twofold reasons. Firstly, according to Orowan strengthening, the finer and more uniformly dispersing the particles, the stronger the composites. Secondly, well-dispersed particulate fillers can impose resistance to grain boundaries (GBs) migration and thus enhanced thermal stability by the Zener pinning mechanism. However, in PRCMCs, these particulate fillers tend to agglomerate or segregate along the GBs, leading to localized stress concentrations and results in the premature failure [3]. Even worse, high-density particles segregated to GBs regions generate phase-matrix galvanic cells, along with accumulated dislocations at these sites, promoting corrosion rates of composites in corrosive environments [4].

To overcome the above dilemma, one feasible method is to manipulate intragranular distribution of reinforcements. The intragranular nano-particulate can relieve stress localization and elevate GBs stability by enhancing deformation compatibility between adjacent grains [5]. Additionally, the reduction in the reinforcement particle size also reduces stress concentration at the particle corners and leads to a consequential increase in work hardenability due to reinforcement-dislocation interactions, which is conducive to obtain impressive mechanical properties. For instance, Li et al. [6] introduced the carbon nanoparticles inside Cu ultrafine-grained matrix and found these intragranular nanoparticles activated multiple hardening mechanisms, thus significantly enhanced the strength-plasticity compatibility. Moreover, compared to intergranular phases, intragranular second-phase may avoid galvanic corrosion at GBs that bring hope to improve composites corrosion resistance. However, it is challenging to achieve intragranular distribution of reinforcements due to the low migration capacity of GBs during powder metallurgy. In that case, the large size reinforcements cannot be separated from GBs and remain within the grain interior because them could further inhibit the GBs migration rate via significant Zener pinning force [7]. Promisingly, some pioneering studies have demonstrated that small sized particles are more likely to be located in grain interiors via GBs rotation and migration mechanisms during sintering process [7,8].

A zero-dimensional carbon material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), utilized as an innovative reinforcement. It's diameter less than 10 nm and possesses few-layer graphite structures, exhibiting similar chemical and physical properties to graphene [9]. Besides compare with traditional ceramic or metallic oxide particles (e.g., TiC [10], and Y2O3 [11]), GQDs nanoparticles have better dispersibility owing to abundant surface groups and continuous Brownian motion in solution [8]. Moreover, extremely fine sized carbon nanoparticles facilitate the manipulation within Cu grain interiors, it has displayed attractive strengthening efficiency for metal composites [6]. Motivated by these considerations, current study successfully synthesized intragranular GQDs clusters-reinforced Cu composites via a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy. A molecular-level mixing (MLM) synthesis enabled the uniform distribution of extremely fine carbon nanoparticles in Cu matrix. Results demonstrated that GQDs/Cu composites possessed superior mechanical properties and high corrosion resistance. The effects of GQDs clusters on the microstructure, as well as the strengthening mechanism for the enhanced mechanical properties and corrosion resistance were evaluated and discussed.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized by nitric acid reflux method [12]. Specifically, 2 g multiwalled CNTs (with density: ∼ 2.1 g/cm3) was combined with 250 mL HNO3 in a round-bottom flask. The mixed suspension was then heated at 140 °C and refluxed for 60 h. The solution then was neutralized by the dropwise add of NaOH solution, and underwent dialysis purification to remove salt impurities. Finally, purified GQDs were obtained as expected by freeze-drying.

The “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy used for fabricating GQDs/Cu powders via molecular-level mixing (MLM) method had demonstrated in our previous paper [8]. The as-fabricated GQDs/Cu powders were then converted into flake powder through vacuum ball milling (BM). Finally, the composite powders were densified by spark plasma sintering (SPS) at 600 °C for 5 min with applied pressure of 200 MPa. The content of GQDs clusters in composites was 0, 0.1 wt% and 0.2 wt%. A preparation schematic illustration of GQDs/Cu composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

The microstructures of powders and consolidated bulks were researched by field emission electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300), electron back scattered diffraction (EBSD), and transmission electron microscope (TEM, TecnaiG2-TF30). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, 200 F3 Netzsch) experiments were utilized to evaluate the grain boundary energy (GBE) of bulk composites. Vickers microhardness of samples was tested using an MC010 microhardness analysis system with a load of 0.98 N and an application time of 15 s. The mechanical properties of bulk materials were tested with a universal testing machine at a tensile rate of 0.18 mm min−1. Regarding electrochemical testing, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and potential polarization were conducted on the electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Huachen CHI660E). The utilized corrosive system was a standard three electrode system, and a typical NaCl aqueous solution (3.5 wt%) was used as the electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs nanoparticles and composite powders
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of as-synthesized GQDs is depicted in Fig. 2 (a-b), illustrating a near-spherical microstructure with an average size of ∼3.07 nm. HRTEM image indicates distinct lattice stripes of GQDs with {10
1 ¯
0} interplanar distance of 0.21 nm. The surface functional groups of GQDs were characterized using FTIR (in Fig. 2(c)). Typical CC (∼1637 cm−1), N-C=C (1404 cm−1), C-H (1346 cm−1) were detected [12]. The morphologies of GQDs/Cu powders during each preparation procedure were characterized by SEM (Fig. 2(d-f)). Firstly, the GQDs/Cu2O powders prepared by MLM exhibits regular cube structures (Fig. 2(d)). After reducing under H2, powders surface developed numerous holes due to the diffusion and escape courses of H2O atoms (Fig. 2(e)). In order to eliminate these holes and improve the sintering densification, and further promote the uniform dispersion of GQDs clusters, the reduced GQDs/Cu powders had transformed into lamellar flake by vacuum BM, the morphology of deformed GQDs/Cu lamellar powders is presented in Fig. 2(f).

Grain characteristics of pure Cu and GQDs/Cu composites were analyzed using EBSD images (Fig. 3 (a-b)) and optical micrograph (Fig. S1). Both pure Cu and composites exhibit coarsened, equiaxed grains with randomized orientations. The average size of pure Cu is ∼3.4 μm (see Fig. 3(c)), but the average sizes decrease to ∼2.27 μm and ∼ 1.4 μm for 0.1 GQDs/Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composites, respectively. It indicates that introducing GQDs clusters into Cu matrix could significantly refine grains size. This is mainly attributed to GQDs clusters could be used as the nucleation sites of Cu2O particles during MLM process, which promoting the heterogeneous nucleation of Cu2O particles and then refining the starting grain size of GQDs/Cu2O powders, resulted in refined the grain size of composites. Notably, the 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite contains a higher concentration of twin domains (in Fig. 3(e)). The twin domain area rates of pure Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite are 8.56% and 17.04%, respectively. The typical twin morphologies were shown in the Supporting Information (Fig. S2). Additionally, the length and thickness of twin domains in pure Cu are 2.42 μm and 1.06 μm (see Fig. S3), but the length and thickness of twin domains in 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite are only 1.84 μm and 0.39 μm, respectively.

Prior studies have reported that the deforming twin boundaries (TBs) configuration would improve the strength of Cu composites by effectively impeding dislocations movement and without critically lose its high electrical conductivity due to the electrical resistivity of TBs is about one order of magnitude lower than that of conventional high angle GBs. [13]. Meanwhile, Han et al. [14] reported the existence of twin domains could reduce GBE and stabilize the GBs structures. Herein, the effects of GQDs clusters addition and high content twin domains on GBE were quantificationally calculated by DSC measurements, and results are displayed in Fig. 3 (f). The GBE of 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite was calculated to be 0.12 J/m2, which is ∼40% less than that of pure Cu (0.2 J/m2). The calculated value of pure Cu in this research is consistent with others reported coherent interface, which ranged from 0.005 to 0.2 J/m2 [15]. Detailed calculative process of present study is provided in the Supporting Information (Text 1). While this is a somewhat arbitrary definition, it can still be reasonably indicated that the GQDs clusters introduced can reduce the GBE, thereby enhancing the GBs thermal stability in 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite.

Fig. 4(a) exhibits that the twin domains mainly formed around GQDs clusters, as shown by white arrows for twins and yellow arrows for GQDs clusters. It is believed that the composite with more twin domains is derived from the rise of local stress field introduced by GQDs clusters addition. The critical stress for twinning can be quantified by the Eq. [16]:
(1)
τ crit
=
2
3
Nb twin
3 N
4
− 1
γ ut
+
2
γ tsf
+
γ isf
2
−
2
3 N
γ us
+
γ isf
b twin
where
τ crit
is the critical shear stress for twinning, N is the number of layers for twin nucleation, which tends to be 3 for the classic smallest twin layers [17].
b twin
is the Burgers vector of partial dislocation and the value is 0.147 nm for Cu [18]. 2
γ tsf
is the twin stacking fault energy,
γ isf
is the intrinsic stacking fault energy,
γ us
is the unstable stacking fault energy, and
γ ut
is the unstable twin energy. The value of 2
γ tsf
,
γ isf
,
γ us
, and
γ ut
for Cu are 42 mj/m2, 46 mj/m2, 232 mj/m2, and 253 mj/m2, respectively [19]. Therefore, the critical shear stress for twinning can be calculated to be 141 MPa. While, the resolved shear stress of pure Cu and composites of present study can be calculated by the Schmid law [20]:
(2)
τ =
σ
M T
where τ is the true resolved shear stress, σ is the true stress, and
M T
is the Taylor factor. The true stress for Cu and composites is in the Supporting Information (Fig. S4), and the value of
M T
is 3.06 for Cu [21]. From Eq. (2), the resolved shear stress of 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite and pure Cu can be calculated to be 149 MPa and 121 MPa, respectively. Such result shown a much higher stress field was generated in composite due to the GQDs clusters addition, even higher than the critical stress of twinning. In brief, the composite has greater advantages to form twin domains compared to pure Cu, which leads to prominent difference in twin density for Cu and composite.

TEM images (Fig. 4(b)) demonstrate the near-spherical GQDs clusters are uniformly distributed in Cu matrix, as indicated by yellow arrow. The GQDs clusters are formed through agglomeration of several GQDs nanoparticles owing to thermal activation migration during the preparation of composites and their average diameter is 16.8 nm (in Fig. 4(c)). These intragranular dispersion can be ascribed that GQDs clusters could be migrate into Cu grain interiors via GBs rotation and migration mechanisms during SPS process [7]. Moreover, HRTEM image shows a clean and close-knit interface feature between GQDs cluster and Cu matrix (in Fig. 4(d)), suggesting robust interface bonding. The measured interplanar distance of the GQDs and Cu matrix are 0.18 nm and 0.21 nm, corresponding to the spacing of (01
1 ¯
2 ¯
) and (111) planes, respectively (in Fig. 4(e-f)). Furthermore, less dislocation feature is observed along the Cu-GQDs interfaces (Fig. 4(e-f)), indicating negligible strain mismatch surrounding the region, which would prevent significant stress/strain corrosion.

Fig. 5(a) exhibits the representative engineering stress-strain curves of pure Cu and composites. 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite exhibits the highest yield strength (YS) of 376 MPa and ultra-tensile strength (UTS) of 407 MPa, which are 103% and 41% higher than those of pure Cu (YS: 185 MPa, UTS:288 MPa). Meanwhile, this composite still maintains a moderate elongation of 20%, demonstrating a remarkable strengthening effect exerted by the intragranular GQDs clusters. The strengthening efficiency (
S eff
) of GQDs clusters in Cu matrix can be calculated by [22]:
(3)
S eff
=
σ c
−
σ m
V GQDs
σ m
where
σ c
and
σ m
are the yield strength of composite and Cu matrix, respectively;
V GQDs
is the volume fraction of GQDs.
V GQDs
can be obtained by [22]:
(4)
V GQDs
=
W GQDs
W GQDs
+
ρ GQDs
ρ Cu
−
ρ GQDs
ρ Cu
W GQDs
where
W GQDs
is mass fraction of GQDs;
ρ GQDs
(2.1 g/cm3) and
ρ Cu
(8.9 g/cm3) are the density of GQDs and Cu matrix. The calculated
S eff
values for 0.1 GQDs/Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composites are 228 and 129, respectively.

Microstructure of the post-deformed 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite was studied to explore the mechanical strengthening mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5(c), abundant dislocations located within the grain interior, which relieves dislocations sinking near GBs regions and would also avoid interface separation [23]. It is considered that intragranular GQDs clusters changed the stress partitioning and fracture modes, thus enhancing the strength and ductility of composite. Meanwhile, it is noteworthy that dislocation lines are trapped by GQDs clusters (Fig. 5(d)), leading to dislocations multiplication in grain interior [5]. Moreover, marked pronounced dislocation loop is detected around GQDs cluster via the effective interaction of movable dislocations with GQDs cluster (Fig. 5(e)), which is a feature of significant Orowan strengthening [24]. As set forth, GQDs cluster addition could bring about high content of twin domains, TBs act as a special kind of coherent boundary and hinder the dislocation motion availably. Briefly, benefit from the significant grain refinement (
σ GR
), the Orowan strengthening (
σ Orowan
), the dislocations strengthening ( ∆
σ D
), and the twin strengthening brought via the addition of GQDs cluster (
σ Twin
), endowing the GQDs/Cu composites with superior mechanical properties. These reinforcing contributions caused via the reinforcements can be investigated as expressed:
(5)
σ c
=
σ m
+ Δ
σ GR
+
σ Orowan
+ Δ
σ D
+
σ Twin

Where
σ m
and
σ c
are the YS of pure Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite, respectively. Their calculation values are 22, 63, 70, 36 MPa, respectively (the specific calculation process is described in Supporting Materials (Text 2)).

The radar image in Fig. 5(b) displays various properties comparison, with detailed statistics shown in Table 1 . The electricity conductivity of 0.1 GQDs/Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composites are 90.3 %IACS and 89.6 %IACS, respectively, which are close to pure Cu (92.5 %IACS). It has emphasized the intragranular distribution GQDs clusters have negligible effect on the intrinsic electron migration behaviors. Additionally, from the aspect of composite microstructure, there are much higher twin density in composites. Due to the electrical resistivity of coherent TBs is about one order of magnitude lower than that of conventional high angle GBs [25], it would equilibrate the electron scattering resulted from GBs. On the other words, the clean and substantial interface between intragranular GQDs clusters and Cu matrix effectively minimizes the resistivity caused by interface scattering and boundary scattering, providing a “freeway” for electron transportation with less charge loss in the grains. Thus, the high strength, plasticity and good electrical conductivity synergy set GQDs/Cu composites apart from the previously reported Cu-based materials, offering novel insights into the design of advanced structural-functional integrated composites.

Fig. 6 (a-b) display the Bode plots of pure Cu and Cu composites. The impedance modulus value |Z| at low frequency reflects the corrosion resistance and the phase angle is used to evaluate the capacity to hinder the penetration of the electrolyte materials [26]. The relatively greater |Z| value and phase angle of 0.2 GQDs/Cu demonstrate the superior corrosion resistance. As is well known, the electrochemical corrosion of metallic materials is connected with the formation of protective oxide film and corrosion. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is utilized to understand the anti-corrosion mechanism (Fig. 6(c)). The curves of samples exhibit similar characteristics, which correspond to the charge transfer of Cu to Cu2+ at the corrosion surface and adsorption of corrosion products at the exposed surface [27,28]. The equivalent circuit is presented in Fig. 6(c), and the fitting parameters are listed in supporting Information (Table S1).
R s
is the electrolyte solution resistance;
R
ct 1
and
R
ct 2
are charge transfer resistance;
CPE 1
and
CPE 2
are oxide film capacitance and the interface capacitance associated with pitting, respectively. The low electrical resistance of pure Cu (R
ct1 = 581.7, R
ct2 = 2987 Ωcm2) indicates that the passive oxide film of pure Cu is porous and incompact. In contrast, 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite has larger charge transfer resistance (R
ct1 = 822.6, R
ct2 = 7825 Ωcm2), resulting greater protection against corrosion. Moreover, the radius of capacitive arcs reflects the corrosion resistance, the larger radius represents better corrosion resistance.

In order to quantify corrosion resistance, polarization curves were tested and shown in Fig. 6(d). As indicated, the synthesized GQDs/Cu composites have a lager corrosion potential (E
corr ) than pure Cu, implying that composites have enhanced corrosion resistance. Additionally, the corrosion current density (I
corr ) shows a gradual reduction in order of pure Cu, 0.1 GQDs/Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu. This result reflects that intragranular GQDs clusters contribute to postpone corrosion kinetics. The corrosion rate (CR, mm/year) can be calculated using the Eq. [29]:
(6)
CR =
kMI corr
ρ m
where
k
is a constant 3270 mol/A, M
is molecular weight (64 g/mol), and
ρ m
is the density. Table 2 lists the electrochemical parameters obtained from the Tafel curves. The corrosion rate of 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite was the smallest, which reduced by 31.2% compared to pure Cu.

SEM analysis was implemented in order to determine the corroded surface characteristics of pure Cu and 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite after the same corrosion condition are shown in Fig. 7 (a-b). The corroded GBs and deep corrosion holes are clearly visible in the pure Cu surface, indicating the corrosion mainly spread along the GBs direction and relative low corrosion resistance caused partial exfoliation (Fig. 7(a)). In contrast, 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite displays a relative flat corroded surface, with no corrosion exfoliation (Fig. 7(b)), further confirming composites have better corrosion resistance.

The corresponding corrosion mechanisms are schematically shown in Fig. 7(c-d). Firstly, SEM analysis has demonstrated that the corrosion mainly spread along GBs direction, thus elevating GBs stability will benefit to suppress corrosion process. Basing on EBSD observation and DSC measurements (as shown in Fig. 3), the addition of GQDs clusters reduced the GBE (∼40%) and stabilized GBs structures via forming more twin domains in composites. Such interface structure delays the corrosion media propagation along GBs. Meanwhile, extremely fine sized GQDs clusters distributed within Cu grains could effectively relieve the stress concentration at GBs (the segregation of large particle gathered at GBs usually cause stress concentration), as a result, significant corrosion exfoliation or holes cannot be observed on composites corrosion surface. Secondly, manipulating GQDs clusters into grain interiors to design microstructures that are intrinsically electrochemically homogeneous (Fig. 7(d)), which can not only weaken severe phase-matrix galvanic cells corrosion but obstruct the transmission path of corrosion media. Furthermore, the microstructure refinement would aid in the formation of a stable protected oxide film, which in turn mitigates the corrosion process via enhancing the charge transfer resistance [28,30].

In summary, a “nanodispersion-in-grains” strategy was conducted to synthesized GQDs/Cu composites for achieving superior mechanical-electrical performances, along with high corrosion resistance performances. 0.2 GQDs/Cu composite demonstrated the tensile strength and elongation of 407 MPa and 20%, respectively. Concurrently, compared with pure Cu, the corrosion rate of 0.2 GQDs/Cu was reduced by 31.2%. The uniformly dispersed intragranular GQDs clusters refined grain size, as well as promoted significant dislocations multiplication in grain interior and Orowan strengthening via dislocation-nanoparticle interaction. Meanwhile, the twin domains greatly enhanced GBs structural stability, accompanied with a stable protected oxide film formed in composites, which synergistically restrained the corrosion reaction and endowed GQDs/Cu composites with satisfactory corrosion resistance. This work carried meaningful implications for understanding the effect of intragranular GQDs clusters on the mechanical and corrosion properties, which can be expected to widen the applications of structural-functional integrative Cu composites.

Wenfu Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Rui Bao: Project administration, Methodology. Caiju Li: Formal analysis. Xiaofeng Chen: Writing – review & editing. Junjun Pai: Methodology, Conceptualization. Chunxuan Liu: Validation. Qiwen Liang: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 52174345, 52064032). Major Science and Technology Projects of Yunnan Province (Grant No. 202202AG050004). Huxiang High-level Talent Gathering Project (2022RC4019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchar.2024.114240.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigating the effects of hybrid reinforcement particles on the microstructural, mechanical and tribological properties of friction stir processed copper surface composites [J]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Thankachan, K.S. Prakash. V. Kavimani. Investigating the effects of hybrid reinforcement particles on the microstructural, mechanical and tribological properties of friction stir processed copper surface composites [J], Composites Part B: Engineering. 174 (2019) 107057. doi: 10.1016/j.compositesb.2019.107057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ceramic particles reinforced copper matrix composites manufactured by advanced powder metallurgy: preparation, performance, and mechanisms [J]

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.F. Yan, S.Q. Kou, H.U. Yang, et al. Ceramic particles reinforced copper matrix composites manufactured by advanced powder metallurgy: preparation, performance, and mechanisms [J], International Journal of Extreme Manufacturing. 5 (2023) 032006. doi: 10.1088/2631-7990/acdb0b.
